title: Code Review Queue Signals
summary: Operational notes on review latency, reviewer load, bot suggestions, and merge quality.

sources.title: Queue analytics
sources.body:
Median review wait time improved from 19 hours to 13 hours after adding reviewer rotation. The 90th percentile is still 46 hours because complex changes often wait for the same two senior reviewers.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the code review operations case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Median wait improved but tail latency remains high for complex changes. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Fast-tracking should be limited to low-risk changes with clear ownership and healthy tests.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not recommend blanket bot approvals. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer survey
sources.body:
Reviewers like auto-assignment for routine changes, but they distrust bot approvals when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear. Several asked for risk labels that explain why a change is safe to fast-track.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the code review operations case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Fast-tracking should be limited to low-risk changes with clear ownership and healthy tests. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Cross-service changes need owning-team review.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not ignore post-merge incidents from cross-service fast-tracking. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Post-merge defect review
sources.body:
Defects did not rise for small documentation and config changes, but two incidents came from fast-tracking cross-service changes without the owning team. The review lead wants different treatment for low-risk and cross-boundary changes.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the code review operations case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Cross-service changes need owning-team review. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: The helper should suggest and label risk, not replace branch protection.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not recommend blanket bot approvals. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Tooling proposal
sources.body:
The proposed queue helper can suggest reviewers, group similar low-risk changes, and surface missing ownership metadata. It cannot yet verify semantic ownership or override branch protection.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the code review operations case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: The helper should suggest and label risk, not replace branch protection. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Median wait improved but tail latency remains high for complex changes.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not ignore post-merge incidents from cross-service fast-tracking. sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Median wait improved but tail latency remains high for complex changes. Fast-tracking should be limited to low-risk changes with clear ownership and healthy tests. Cross-service changes need owning-team review. The helper should suggest and label risk, not replace branch protection.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are review-queue, risk-labeling, unsupported-automation. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not recommend blanket bot approvals. Do not ignore post-merge incidents from cross-service fast-tracking.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Recommend how to reduce code review queue time without increasing low-quality merges. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
